Name: Volunteer Shift Scheduler
Description: Coverage status, volunteer load (under/ok/over), and event-level A–F staffing grade all fall out automatically.

Events.Description: Table: Events — top-level container (a festival, food bank shift, conference, etc.).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display name of the event.

schema.name: ShiftCount
schema.Description: Total shifts attached to this event.

schema.name: TotalSlotsNeeded
schema.Description: Sum of needed-count across all shifts in this event.

schema.name: TotalSlotsFilled
schema.Description: Sum of filled-count across all shifts in this event.

schema.name: CoveragePercent
schema.Description: TotalSlotsFilled / TotalSlotsNeeded (0..1).

schema.name: StaffingGrade
schema.Description: A-F letter grade from CoveragePercent. A: >=95%, B: >=85%, C: >=70%, D: >=50%, F: below.
schema.explanation_rich: **The event-level rollup of every per-shift constraint.** Each shift's `FilledCount` and `NeededCount` aggregate into `TotalSlotsFilled` / `TotalSlotsNeeded`, divide into `CoveragePercent`, then bucket into an A-F grade. Worked example: *Harvest Festival* needs 14 total slots across 4 shifts and has 12 filled — `CoveragePercent` is 0.857, which lands in the `>=0.85` bucket, so `StaffingGrade = B`. Fill one more slot on any understaffed shift (Setup Morning or Food Service Afternoon) and the festival's grade promotes to `A` automatically — the cascade reaches all the way from a single new `Assignments` row to the event's headline letter.

Volunteers.Description: Table: Volunteers — people available to be slotted into shifts.
Volunteers.summary_rich: The **availability side of the constraint**. Each volunteer has a `MaxHours` ceiling and a `ReliabilityScore`. `AssignedHours` rolls up from their `Assignments`; `LoadStatus` (under / ok / over) is the live verdict on whether the coordinator has slotted them well. Push someone past `MaxHours` and they flip to `over` before you close the editor.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display name.

schema.name: EmailAddress
schema.Description: Used as the click-to-login identity.

schema.name: ReliabilityScore
schema.Description: 0..1 — coordinator's confidence the volunteer will show up. Drives per-shift coverage weighting.

schema.name: MaxHours
schema.Description: Volunteer's stated maximum hours for this scheduling period.

schema.name: AssignmentCount
schema.Description: Number of shifts this volunteer is assigned to.

schema.name: AssignedHours
schema.Description: Sum of DurationHours across this volunteer's assigned shifts.

schema.name: LoadStatus
schema.Description: under | ok | over. Under: <50% of MaxHours. Over: >100%. Otherwise ok.
schema.explanation_rich: **The per-volunteer availability constraint.** `AssignedHours` aggregates `DurationHours` across every shift this volunteer is slotted into; the rule compares it against the volunteer's stated `MaxHours` ceiling. Worked example: **Sam Park** has `MaxHours = 8` but is currently assigned to three Harvest shifts totalling 10 hours — so `AssignedHours` (10) exceeds `MaxHours` (8) and `LoadStatus` resolves to `over`. Drop one of Sam's three assignments and the same field flips back to `ok` the same instant. Casey Cole, by contrast, sits at `AssignedHours = 0` and resolves to `under` — the coordinator's hint that there's slack to absorb a re-shuffle.

Shifts.Description: Table: Shifts — a time block within an event needing N people with a required skill.
Shifts.summary_rich: The **unit of constraint**. Each shift is a hard coverage requirement: N volunteers, a required skill tag, a duration. `FilledCount` is the live tally from `Assignments`; `CoverageStatus` is the constraint satisfaction verdict (understaffed / covered / overstaffed). The coordinator's whole job is to drive every shift to `covered` without pushing any volunteer's load past `MaxHours`.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Short label (e.g. "Setup Morning").

schema.name: Event
schema.Description: Parent event.

schema.name: RequiredSkill
schema.Description: Free-text skill tag (e.g. "setup", "intake", "food-handler").

schema.name: DurationHours
schema.Description: Length of the shift in hours; drives per-volunteer load.

schema.name: NeededCount
schema.Description: How many volunteers the coordinator wants on this shift.

schema.name: FilledCount
schema.Description: Number of volunteers currently assigned.

schema.name: CoverageStatus
schema.Description: understaffed | covered | overstaffed.
schema.explanation_rich: **The per-shift constraint verdict.** Every shift declares a `NeededCount`; every assignment increments `FilledCount` through the `COUNTIFS` aggregation. This field compares the two and labels the gap. Worked example: *Setup Morning* on the Harvest Festival needs 4 volunteers and currently has 3 assignments — Morgan, Sam, Jamie — so `FilledCount` is 3, less than 4, and `CoverageStatus` resolves to `understaffed`. Add an `Assignments` row for Alex on that shift and the same field flips to `covered` on the next read — no application code touched, no recompute button pressed.

Assignments.Description: Table: Assignments — a volunteer slotted into a shift. Join table between Volunteers and Shifts (ERB does not allow direct many-to-many).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label (VolunteerName @ ShiftName).

schema.name: ShiftDurationHours
schema.Description: Pulled through to support per-volunteer AssignedHours aggregation.

_meta.tagline: Coverage requirements, availability windows, skill tags — the constraints solve themselves.
_meta.description_rich: Volunteer Shift Scheduler is a **constraint-satisfaction demo, not a calendar app.** Every shift declares a hard requirement — *N volunteers, this skill tag, this duration*. Every volunteer declares an availability window — *MaxHours, ReliabilityScore*. `Assignments` are the proposed solutions, and the rulebook is the live judge: each shift's `CoverageStatus` (understaffed / covered / overstaffed), each volunteer's `LoadStatus` (under / ok / over), and the event's A–F `StaffingGrade` all fall out automatically. Pin a new index card to the corkboard — add one `Assignments` row — and watch three layers of verdicts recompute on the next read, in every substrate, with no application code in the middle.
use_cases: **Resolve an understaffed shift.** Open *Setup Morning* on the Harvest Festival (`CoverageStatus = understaffed`, 3 of 4 filled), add an `Assignments` row for Casey Cole, and watch the shift flip to `covered` and the event's `StaffingGrade` climb from B toward A.

**Catch an over-booked volunteer.** Sam Park sits at `LoadStatus = over` (10 assigned hours against an 8-hour `MaxHours`). Drop one of Sam's three assignments and the rule flips back to `ok` — the cascade does not need a recompute button.

**Match a required skill to who's actually available.** Each shift carries a `RequiredSkill` tag (`setup`, `intake`, `food-handler`); use it to filter who you can pin to that card before the constraint engine even runs.

**Promote an event's grade by one letter.** Watch how a single new `Assignments` row propagates: `Shifts.FilledCount` → `Events.TotalSlotsFilled` → `Events.CoveragePercent` → `Events.StaffingGrade`. Four aggregations and one bucket function — no JOIN written by hand.
**Read the same coverage answer in two substrates.** Ask *"which shifts are understaffed?"* in the generated Postgres view and again in Python — same answer, no glue code, no drift.
_meta.journal_seed: Two events on the corkboard. Food Bank Saturday is fully staffed (grade A); Harvest Festival is two slots short of an A. Sam is over by two hours; Casey hasn't been pinned to anything yet.
